title: An Argument for Transhumanistic Instrumentality
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jun 02, 2010 11:10 pm As requested by Reichu, an argument for a transhumanistic instrumentality (aka GOOD END) in EoTV. For the TL/DR crowd, the basic idea is as follows: Instrumentality allows people to retain a degree of individuality and their own world. There are many such worlds, enough for each person or group of intermingled persons to have a place to call their own The person or persons within such a world have complete control of its contents, though this control may be unconscious Connection with others can be refused at least to some extent so a degree of privacy is still possible, as is death if people choose to allow themselves to cease existing. So, you want some evidence? It’s all over the place if you just take people’s words literally . Take Ep25’s final scene for example. Shinji is nudged out of his self destructive dreams momentarily into a theatre (symbolizing the falseness of his imprisoning delusions) where the others tell him exactly where he is and what the problem is. Asuka: There are a lot of realities. This is one of them. Misato: This is the result you wished for. Shinji: The one I wished for? Rei: Yes, annihilation, a world where no one is saved. Shinji: No! It's just that no one saved me! No one... RITSUKO: No one can save you. Kaji: This is what you wished for.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Wed Jun 02, 2010 11:42 pm ...

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Wed Jun 02, 2010 11:47 pm NemZ wrote: It’s all over the place if you just take people’s words literally .

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Jun 03, 2010 12:40 am NemZ, I split your post and the follow-ups into a separate topic since it seemed like a good idea. ( Citation to original thread for posterity.) Hope you don't mind. Will provide response later.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jun 03, 2010 1:57 am What NemZ said. In EoTV, after a few early flashbacks and enigmatic glimpses, the old objective order is abandoned. Gendo tells us what he has done -- this is the long trailed world-changing event that was cooking in the background. And, at the end, after Shinji has broken free of his own mind forged manacles, we see Gendo reunited with Yui as part of the welcoming party of friends, colleagues and family -- the master plan run inside the SEELE conspiracy has clearly achieved its desired end. And we're not in Kansas any more. The transformative event has cleared away all the old cruft and clutter, the drag anchor called the Human Condition -- such childish things can be put aside.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 03, 2010 3:33 am Legendary wrote: This is always a bad approach with scenes like EoTV.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jun 03, 2010 7:38 am * Instrumentality allows people to retain a degree of individuality and their own world. * There are many such worlds, enough for each person or group of intermingled persons to have a place to call their own * The person or persons within such a world have complete control of its contents, though this control may be unconscious * Connection with others can be refused at least to some extent so a degree of privacy is still possible, as is death if people choose to allow themselves to cease existing.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 03, 2010 12:38 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Note that this is all before Instrumentality is officially completed.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jun 03, 2010 12:59 pm That isn't a metaphor... it's exactly what happens.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jun 03, 2010 1:38 pm NemZ wrote: Then you're taking it wrong, because he says "here" when he is clearly still in instrumentality.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 03, 2010 1:57 pm I don't know what to tell you folks, but I'm getting tired of the bumpercars routine. Maybe there's a language barrier involved and/or perhaps Anno is failing to keep the 4th wall in place as things come to the end. Yes there are bits that seem out of place and I grant that I don't have all the answers, but the narrative as a whole says repeatedly and clearly that the world is what Shinji himself chooses to make of it. Since the episodes consist of nothing but dialog and abstract imagery I can't begin to think how you can get any narrative meaning at all out of this ending if you don't start out by accepting what the cast says is true. There's nothing else in the show to go on, so either you take it as it is or you end up pulling meaning from elsewhere and imposing preconceived messages where they don't belong. It seems people prefer the later.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Jun 03, 2010 2:12 pm NemZ wrote: take people’s words literally.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Thu Jun 03, 2010 3:27 pm Please, less quote wars.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Jun 03, 2010 3:32 pm Are "quote wars" really all that bad? Considering we're discussing the minutae of a philosophical stance, these kind of semantic quips are somewhat neccessary to gain a full idea over the concepts being presented.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Thu Jun 03, 2010 3:38 pm Yes. Now quit it.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Jun 03, 2010 4:27 pm NemZ wrote: what makes it 'officially' incomplete?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jun 03, 2010 5:09 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Big blue sphere thing is obviously evocative of Earth

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Jun 03, 2010 6:58 pm Instead of addressing all the various nitpicky details and clogging the place up with even more quote-a-thon posts, let's just look at things one at a time, okay? Let's start at the beginning: Why shouldn't we accept that what the characters say is exactly what they mean? legendary wrote: This is always a bad approach with scenes like EoTV.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Thu Jun 03, 2010 6:59 pm I accept what they say is true. I just don't think that they're being literal in the sense you do.

[]:             Quote Post by planet news » Thu Jun 03, 2010 7:48 pm NemZ wrote: That isn't a metaphor... it's exactly what happens. The metaphor comes into play on a higher level of meaning where Anno is using this story to try and tell hikikomori to abandon their lifestyles and let other people in their lives.
